patterns.title: Definite vs indefinite article
patterns.explanation: Use 'a/an' when introducing something new or non-specific. Use 'the' when referring to something already known or unique.

sentences.text: I saw a dog in the park.
I saw the dog in a park.
sentences.error: Use 'a' for a non-specific park and 'a' to introduce the dog for the first time.

sentences.text: The sun rises in the east.
A sun rises in the east.
sentences.error: There is only one sun — use 'the' for unique nouns.

patterns.title: Present perfect vs simple past
patterns.explanation: Use simple past for completed actions with a specific time. Use present perfect for actions with a connection to the present or no specific time.

sentences.text: I have never been to Japan.

sentences.error: Without a specific time frame, use present perfect: 'I have never been'.

sentences.text: She has finished the report yesterday.
sentences.error: 'Yesterday' is a specific time — use simple past, not present perfect.

patterns.title: Second conditional — hypothetical present/future
patterns.explanation: If + past simple, would + infinitive. Used for unlikely or hypothetical situations.
patterns.template: If I ___ more time, I would travel the world.
patterns.hint: Second conditional: if + past simple verb.

patterns.title: Inversion after negative adverbials
patterns.explanation: When a negative adverb (never, rarely, not only) starts a sentence, subject and auxiliary are inverted.

patterns.title: Cleft sentences for emphasis
patterns.explanation: Cleft sentences (It is/was ... that ...) emphasise a particular part of a sentence.

sentences.text: It was the unexpected result that surprised everyone.
It was the unexpected result which surprised everyone.
It was the unexpected result surprised everyone.
sentences.error: A cleft sentence needs 'that' or 'which': 'It was X that/which...'.
